title: Squeaky Brakes
response: Squeaky brakes are usually caused by contaminated brake pads or rotors. Here's how to fix it:
steps: Spray the rotor (disc) with isopropyl alcohol (70%+) and wipe clean with a dry cloth
Check if the brake pads are glazed (shiny surface) — if so, lightly sand them with fine sandpaper
Make sure the caliper is aligned — it should not rub the rotor on one side only
Bed in your brakes: find a safe area and do 10 firm stops from moderate speed to condition new pads

title: Gear Slipping / Chain Skip
response: Chain skipping or gear slipping is usually a cable tension or chain wear issue.
steps: Shift to the middle gear and pedal — if it skips, turn the barrel adjuster (on the derailleur) counter-clockwise by half turns until smooth
Check if your chain is worn using a chain checker tool — if worn past 0.75%, replace the chain
Inspect the cassette cogs for shark-fin shaped teeth — that means the cassette needs replacing too
Check that the derailleur hanger is not bent — even a slight bend causes persistent skipping

title: Flat Tire
response: A flat tire is the most common roadside issue. With the right tools, you can fix it in under 10 minutes.
steps: Remove the wheel: open the quick release or unscrew the thru-axle and disengage the brake if needed
Use tire levers to pry one side of the tire off the rim — never use a screwdriver, it damages the tube
Pull out the inner tube and inflate it slightly to find the puncture — listen/feel for the leak
Patch the tube with a patch kit (sandpaper the area, apply vulcanizing cement, press patch) OR replace with a spare tube
Before re-inserting the tube, check the inside of the tire with your fingers for the object that caused the flat
Re-seat the tire, inflate to correct pressure (MTB: 35–50 PSI), and reinstall the wheel

title: Chain Fell Off
response: A dropped chain is frustrating but easy to fix with your hands (yes, it gets dirty).
steps: Stay calm — stop pedaling immediately when you feel the chain drop
Shift the rear derailleur to the easiest cog (largest) to create slack in the chain
Lift the chain back onto the smallest front chainring (inner ring) by hand while slowly turning the pedals
Once back on, shift through the gears slowly to confirm the chain is seated properly
If the chain drops frequently, your front derailleur limit screws need adjustment — visit a local bike shop

title: Creaking / Clicking Noise
response: Creaking noises while pedaling are almost always a loose or dry component. Finding the source is key.
steps: Check your pedals first — tighten them with a pedal wrench (left pedal is reverse-threaded, righty = lefty for removal)
Check the bottom bracket — grab the cranks and try to wiggle them side to side. Any play = worn BB
Check seat post: remove, clean, apply grease or carbon paste, re-insert and tighten to spec
Check handlebar stem bolts — tighten all bolts evenly in a star pattern
If creak persists, bring to a bike shop — creaking can hide serious problems

title: Weak or Spongy Brakes
response: If your brakes feel spongy or require a lot of lever pull, your brake pads may be worn or your hydraulic system has air bubbles.
steps: Check brake pad thickness — if less than 1mm of pad material remains, replace immediately
For mechanical disc brakes: tighten the cable barrel adjuster on the brake lever until braking is firm
For hydraulic disc brakes: if the lever feels mushy/spongy, you likely need a brake bleed — visit a shop
Check for any leaks in hydraulic brake lines — any mineral oil (or DOT fluid) dripping = urgent repair
NEVER ride with brakes you don't trust — safety first

title: Derailleur / Hanger Bent
response: A bent derailleur hanger is common after a fall or impact and causes phantom shifting problems.
steps: Look from behind the bike — the rear derailleur should hang perfectly parallel to the wheel
If it angles inward or outward, the hanger is bent. Do NOT ride — a bent hanger can snap the derailleur into the spokes
Derailleur hanger alignment tools exist for the DIY fix, but for beginners, bring to a bike shop immediately
Derailleur hangers are cheap sacrificial parts (₱100–₱400) — always carry one on long rides
If the derailleur itself is bent (not just the hanger), it may need replacement

title: Tire Pressure Guide
response: Correct tire pressure dramatically affects ride comfort, speed, and puncture resistance.
steps: MTB on trails: 25–35 PSI (lower = more grip on loose ground)
MTB on paved roads: 35–50 PSI (higher = less rolling resistance, faster)
Road bikes: 80–110 PSI depending on tire width and rider weight
General rule: heavier rider = slightly higher pressure
Check tire sidewall — it will show the MIN and MAX PSI for that specific tire
Check pressure before EVERY ride — tires naturally lose 5–10 PSI per week

title: Saddle Pain / Sore Sit Bones
response: Saddle discomfort is the #1 complaint from new cyclists. It gets better as your body adapts, but a proper setup helps enormously.
steps: Make sure your saddle height is correct: at the bottom of the pedal stroke, your leg should be almost fully extended (5–10 degree bend)
Start with shorter rides (30–45 min) and gradually increase — sit bones need 2–4 weeks to toughen up
Invest in padded cycling shorts (bib shorts are best) — even a cheap pair makes a huge difference
Apply chamois cream to the chamois pad inside your shorts before long rides
Saddle tilt matters: slight downward nose tilt can relieve perineal pressure
If pain persists after 4 weeks of riding, consider a saddle fitting at a local bike shop

title: Chain Lubrication
response: A well-lubed chain is a quiet, efficient chain. Here's how to do it right.
steps: Clean the chain first using a chain cleaning tool + degreaser, or wipe with a dry cloth for light maintenance
Apply lubricant drop by drop to each chain link's inner rollers while slowly backpedaling
Use WET lube for rainy conditions; DRY lube for dry/dusty conditions
After applying, let it soak for 5 minutes, then wipe off all excess lube with a dry rag — excess attracts dirt
Re-lube every 100–150 km (dry conditions) or after every ride in rain
In the Philippines, wet lube is recommended most of the year due to humidity and occasional rain
